home.home::h6zsfyk:
A knot, mathematically, is a closed loop embedded in three-dimensional space.
Take a piece of string, tangle it up, and glue the ends together — that’s a knot.
Two knots are equivalent if you can deform one into the other without cutting or
passing the string through itself. The fundamental problem of knot theory is determining
when two knots are equivalent and when they are not.
home.home::h1tyn39y:
This is harder than it sounds. Even the question “is this knot actually knotted, or
can it be untangled into a simple circle?” has no obvious answer in general.
The tools developed to attack this problem — polynomial invariants, homological algebra,
quantum groups — connect knot theory to areas throughout mathematics and physics.
